turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has our opinion this morning in case 15-1500, Lewis versus Clarke.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Petitioners Brian and Michelle Lewis were driving on a Connecticut interstate when they were struck from behind by a vehicle driven by respondent William Clarke, a Mohegan Tribal Gaming Authority employee, who was transporting Mohegan Sun Casino patrons. Lewis versus Clarke is not a joke. The Lewises brought a negligence suit against Clarke in Connecticut State Court. Clarke moved to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, arguing that because he was an employee of the Gaming Authority, an arm of the Mohegan Tribe, entitled to sovereign immunity, and was acting within the scope of his employment at the time of the accident, he was similarly entitled to sovereign immunity against suit. He also argued in the alternative that he should prevail because the Gaming Authority was bound by tribal law to indemnify him. The tribal court denied Clarke's motion, but the Supreme Court of Connecticut reversed, holding that tribal sovereign immunity barred the suit because Clarke was acting within the scope of his employment when the accident occurred. We granted certiorari to consider whether tribal sovereign immunity bars the Lewises' suit against Clarke and we now reverse the judgment of the Supreme Court of Connecticut. We hold that in a suit brought against a tribal employee in his individual capacity, the employee not the tribe is the real party in interest and the tribe's sovereign immunity is not implicated. That an employee was acting within the scope of his employment at the time the tort was committed is not on its own sufficient to bar a suit against that employee on the basis of sovereign immunity. This ruling aligns with our precedent in the context of lawsuits against state and federal employees or entities where we have said that courts must look to whether the sovereign is the real party in interest to determine whether sovereign immunity bars a suit. We have said that when an employee is sued in his official capacity, the relief sought is only nominally against the official and in fact is against the official's office and thus the sovereign itself. Personal capacity suits, on the other hand, such as the one against Clarke, seek to impose individual liability upon a government officer for actions taken under color of law. Sovereign immunity does not as a general matter bar suits imposing individual liability. These established principles foreclose Clarke's immunity defense. This is a negligence action arising from a tort committed by Clarke on an interstate highway within the State of Connecticut. The judgment will not operate against the tribe or its instrumentalities. And Clarke, not the tribe, is the real party in interest. We hold further that an indemnification provision does not extend a tribe's sovereign immunity where it otherwise would not reach. A voluntary indemnification provision such as the one here does not alter that it is Clarke and not the tribe that is legally bound by the state court's adverse judgment. The tribe's indemnification provision does not convert the suit against Clarke into a suit against the sovereign. When Clarke is sued in his individual capacity, he is held responsible only for his individual wrongdoing. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Supreme Court of Connecticut and the case is remanded for further proceedings not inconsistent with this opinion. Justices Thomas and Ginsburg have each filed opinions concurring in the Judgment. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
